checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully reproduces the text content of the source PDF. Each paragraph and sentence in the JSON has a clear basis in the original document across all four pages. There are no fabricated introductions, conclusions, or section headings.

All contact information, including addresses and case numbers, matches the source PDF exactly. The address for the Respondent's attorney (P.O. Box 1351, Raleigh, North Carolina 27602) and the location of the hearing (1711 New Hope Church Road, Raleigh, North Carolina 27609) are correctly transcribed.

The output includes all sections (Appearances, Issue, Applicable Statutes and Rules, Findings of Fact, Conclusions of Law, Proposal for Decision, and Notice and Order) and all four pages from the source PDF.

The document contains one structural table representing the case caption box on page 1. The JSON represents this correctly with the expected cell contents (Petitioner/Respondent info on the left, document title on the right). No data rows are missing.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text within each section is complete. Long lists like the Findings of Fact (17 items) and Conclusions of Law (5 items) are fully captured without omission.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output preserves the wording and formatting (like italics for 'pro se' and 'prima facie') of the original. It even correctly preserves a typo present in the original document (Item 15: 'Petition previously worked' instead of 'Petitioner'). No meaningful text differences were found.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the entire JSON output and compared it with the source PDF. Each paragraph, list item, and heading appears exactly once in the output, corresponding to its single appearance in the source document. No content from the footer, headers, or repeated sections has been erroneously duplicated.

The document uses Heading Level 2 (H2) for all major section titles (e.g., 'APPEARANCES', 'ISSUE', 'FINDINGS OF FACT'). While there is no H1 tag within the section elements, the document title is correctly captured in the metadata. The hierarchy is flat but consistent, with no skipped levels or illogical nesting.

The conversion correctly identifies the case caption box on page 1 as a table. The structure (1 row, 2 columns) accurately reflects the visual layout where the parties are on the left and the document title is on the right. Headers and data are appropriately handled for this layout.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical flow of the source document perfectly, proceeding from the header/caption through the various sections (Appearances, Issue, Statutes, Findings, Conclusions) to the final signature block on page 4.

The numbered lists under 'FINDINGS OF FACT' and 'CONCLUSIONS OF LAW' are correctly converted into 'ordered' list structures. The numbering and item boundaries match the source. Finding of Fact #3, which includes a sub-block of text for the conviction details, is correctly merged into a single list item content block.
The source document contains no hyperlinks. The JSON output correctly contains no links, ensuring no link injection has occurred.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully reproduces the visual organization of the source PDF. The header information, case caption box, and subsequent sections (Appearances, Issue, Findings of Fact, etc.) are presented in the same logical order and relative positioning as the original document. While the fonts and precise alignment differ slightly due to the nature of HTML, the visual flow and intent are preserved.

The document contains a case caption box on page 1 which functions like a table. In the HTML rendering, this is correctly represented as a bordered container with the Petitioner/Respondent info on the left and the document title on the right, maintaining the visual structure and readability of the source.
The source PDF is a text-based legal document and does not contain any meaningful images, logos, or diagrams. Therefore, no images are missing from the output.

A thorough review of the HTML screenshot confirms that there is no unintended duplication of body content. Section headings appear in the Table of Contents and then once in the document body, which is standard accessible design and not considered a defect.
